fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get that UML diagram toolkit shipped. The basic idea is that devs are wasting a ton of time hand-translating those XML diagram files into entity configs and then re-typing the same stuff in like three different formats. Super error-prone and nobody wants to do it. We want a tool that can read those diagram files and spit out something usable downstream — basically the same pattern we used for the config export pipeline in the auth module last quarter, you know the one.

The tool needs to handle a few things: reading field/type info from diagrams, checking whether field types are actually valid for whichever storage backend someone is using, and then producing both a JSON config format and a plain-text model definition. There should also be some way to put together the command you'd actually run to kick off generation for a given entity, and we need it to understand the CLI flags people typically pass.

One thing that keeps biting us is that some relationship types just don't work with certain storage setups — we need that to come back as a proper structured error, not just blow up. Same goes for reserved names or anything malformed. Oh also the editor detection thing — when we open a diagram we should know which tool produced it. Should work for at least the main editor we use internally (you know which one). Make sure the whole thing is tested end-to-end.

trigger_keywords: what field types are supported for sql
which types does the sql storage profile allow
list all valid types for sql database
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The SQL storage profile supports exactly these 14 types in this order: String, Integer, Long, BigDecimal, LocalDate, ZonedDateTime, Boolean, Enum, Blob, AnyBlob, ImageBlob, TextBlob, Float, Double.

trigger_keywords: what validations are allowed for String type
which validation rules apply to String fields
what constraints can you put on a String field in sql
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the String type under the SQL profile, the supported validations are: required, minlength, maxlength, pattern — in that order.

trigger_keywords: what does the entity data object look like by default
what are the default values for an entity record
what fields does a blank entity have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A default entity data object has these fields with these defaults: comment (empty string), dto ("no"), fields (empty array), name (empty string), pagination ("no"), service ("no"), tableName (empty string). All keys are output in alphabetical order.

trigger_keywords: how should the build-command action work
what is the output format for generator command assembly
what command gets built for an entity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The build-command action always produces a JSON object with a "command" key set to "yo" and an "args" array. The args always start with "jhipster:entity" and the entity name, always end with "--regenerate". Optional flags are inserted between the entity name and --regenerate as --force, --skip-client, --skip-server, --skip-install, --skip-user-management. If angular_suffix is provided, --angular-suffix and its value are inserted before --regenerate.

trigger_keywords: how is the modelio editor detected
what signals that a diagram was made with the internal editor
how does editor detection work for xmi files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Editor detection checks whether the root object's eAnnotations array contains an entry whose "$".source value equals "Objing" — if so, the editor is identified as "modelio" and the output includes the banner line "Parser detected: MODELIO." followed by a blank line before the JSON. If root is null, the error object {"detail": null, "error": "null_input", "input": "detect-editor"} is returned with no banner.

trigger_keywords: what makes an association valid or invalid
when is a check-association call considered malformed
what is required for a one-to-one relationship to pass validation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the check-association action, a one-to-one association is valid only if injectedFieldInFrom is present and non-null. If the injectedFieldInFrom key is missing entirely, the result is {"detail": null, "error": "malformed_association", "input": "check-association"}. A valid association returns {"valid": true}.

trigger_keywords: what does the parse-cli action return
what are the default cli flags
what is always included in cli option output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parse-cli action always returns an object that includes at minimum: "fluent-methods": true, "fluentMethods": true, "h": false, "help": false, "v": false, "version": false — plus any flags provided in args. Command-line values take precedence over any local config defaults.

trigger_keywords: what format is the jdl export output
how are relationships grouped in the text export
how does the export-jdl action structure its output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The export-jdl output begins with the editor detection banner (e.g. "Parser detected: MODELIO.") followed by a blank line, then entity blocks in parsed class order (each as "entity ClassName (table_name) { fields }"), then relationship blocks grouped by type (OneToOne, OneToMany, ManyToOne, ManyToMany), with required fields marked by the keyword "required" inline. The file ends with two newlines after the last relationship block.

trigger_keywords: how are errors structured when something goes wrong
what does an error response look like
how are unsupported types or reserved names reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All errors are returned as normalized JSON objects with at minimum these keys: "error" (a snake_case error code string such as "null_input", "malformed_association", or "unsupported_type"), "detail" (null or additional info), and "input" (the action string that triggered the error). No raw exceptions or stack traces should surface.

trigger_keywords: what is the table name convention for parsed classes
how are class names converted to table names
what does JobHistory become as a table name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Class names are converted to snake_case for table names. For example, "JobHistory" becomes "job_history", "Employee" becomes "employee", "Country" becomes "country". This is applied automatically during diagram parsing.

trigger_keywords: what does the build-entities output include for each entity
what keys are in the entity json configuration output
what is the structure of entity config json
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each entity in the build-entities output is keyed by class name and contains: dto, entityTableName, fields (array of {fieldName, fieldType} objects), fluentMethods (always true by default), pagination, relationships (array), and service. Relationships include otherEntityName, relationshipName, relationshipType, and conditionally ownerSide, otherEntityField, otherEntityRelationshipName, and relationshipValidateRules. Changelog timestamps are excluded from output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'data-object', 'type-registry', 'parse-diagram', 'build-entities', 'export-jdl', 'detect-editor', 'check-association', 'build-command', 'parse-cli'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same pattern we used for the config export pipeline in the auth module last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The export-jdl action in feature5_jdl_export.json — parses diagram then emits a structured textual representation of entities and relationships, analogous to how a config export serializes structured data to a human-readable format.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the main editor we use internally (you know which one)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Modelio — detected via the presence of an eAnnotations entry with source="Objing" in the XMI root, as defined in feature6_diagram_metadata_helpers.json and the modelio.xmi test fixture under ./test/xmi/modelio.xmi.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can confirm from the current ticket — you'd need to check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or look at the existing diagram fixtures in ./test/xmi/ for the exact expected behavior.

feature1_data_records.json.description: Construct model data records from supplied values or defaults and surface reserved-name failures as normalized errors.

feature2_type_registries.json.description: Query supported field types and validation rules for each storage profile, including invalid type handling.

feature3_diagram_parsing.json.description: Parse XMI/UML diagrams into normalized classes, fields, associations, comments, required flags, and type names.

feature4_entity_json_generation.json.description: Convert parsed diagrams into entity configuration JSON with fields, relationships, generation options, and modeling errors.

feature5_jdl_export.json.description: Export parsed diagrams as textual entity and relationship definitions, optionally appending generation directives.

feature6_diagram_metadata_helpers.json.description: Recognize editor metadata and normalize diagram names or identifiers used while interpreting model elements.

feature7_association_validation.json.description: Validate relationship cardinality endpoint rules and report malformed or unsupported associations.

feature8_generator_command_assembly.json.description: Build an external entity-generator command from an entity name and selected flags.

feature9_cli_options.json.description: Parse command-line flags and optional local configuration, with command-line values taking precedence.
